jokes.text: The scavenger hunt clue is written as an earnest rhyming poem referencing pants-pissing: 'We've pissed our pants so many a time, but this place was first and thus sublime.'
jokes.setup: A romantic scavenger hunt clue written in the style of sentimental verse
jokes.punchline: The sentimental memory being commemorated is mutual pants-pissing, delivered with total sincerity
jokes.explanation: The joke works on multiple levels: the formal rhyme scheme creates a comedic contrast with its gross content, and the complete sincerity is pure Ilana. It's character-specific — only Ilana would frame shared pants-pissing as a founding romantic myth of a friendship. The scansion 'first and thus sublime' is a genuinely funny line that earns the 8.

jokes.text: P.S. Dress cute and hot and fresh and young.
jokes.setup: A clue ending with a sincere postscript as if giving a friend fashion advice for a scavenger hunt
jokes.punchline: The escalating list of adjectives — 'cute and hot and fresh and young' — delivered as earnest instruction, the last word landing funny
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — five words doing a lot of work. The quadruple adjective list is funny because each word slightly undercuts the last, ending on 'young,' which implies the others aren't enough. Pure Ilana voice, could not be said by anyone else on any other show.

jokes.text: Abbi's ecstatic reaction to discovering it's a scavenger hunt — 'Scavenger hunt!' followed by gleeful laughter — immediately after she forgot her phone and had to run back
jokes.setup: Abbi reads the clue with building excitement
jokes.punchline: Her pure, childlike joy — 'Forgot my phone. Okay.' — then immediately back into delight, the interruption treating the phone-grab as completely normal
jokes.explanation: A functional comedic beat — the abrupt parenthetical errand mid-ecstasy is a nice small gag. Establishes Abbi's relatable scatter-brain energy. Doesn't reach the level of a fully crafted joke, more of a charming character moment.

jokes.text: Abbi digging through a garbage can to find a clue, reacting with audible disgust: 'Ew...'
jokes.setup: The clue directs her to 'the cleanest can on the block'
jokes.punchline: Visual of Abbi rooting through actual garbage — her 'Ew' underselling the indignity — for the sake of a friendship scavenger hunt
jokes.explanation: Classic physical comedy setup — the 'cleanest can on the block' is already funny as a clue because it implies a hierarchy of garbage cans. The visual payoff is dependable but unremarkable.

jokes.text: The clue references 'a place you got a hickey and I was a player' — Ilana is bragging about giving Abbi a hickey within the romantic mythology of a friendship scavenger hunt clue
jokes.setup: Scavenger hunt clues are supposed to reference sweet shared memories
jokes.punchline: Ilana has enshrined the hickey incident as a key moment in their friendship history, with herself cast as 'the player'
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'I was a player' doing enormous work — Ilana's self-mythology as a sexual operator even within female friendship. The romantic structure of the clue makes the content funnier by contrast.

jokes.text: The clue is a blank piece of paper. The stranger tells Abbi: 'Put it over the fire, you dumb whore.'
jokes.setup: Abbi receives what appears to be a blank clue — 'This is the whole clue? It's a blank piece of paper.'
jokes.punchline: A random stranger casually explains the invisible ink trick, punctuating the helpful advice with 'you dumb whore' — the insult delivered as a natural extension of the instruction
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The misdirection is tight: blank paper → confusion → helpful stranger explains → 'you dumb whore.' The insult's casualness is the entire joke. It's a perfect economy joke — four words doing the maximum work. Ilana has coached this stranger to deliver the line, which adds a layer of delight on rewatch. Quotability is extremely high.

jokes.text: A stranger — clearly prepped by Ilana — tells Abbi 'You know, you're a sexy little bitch.' when she arrives for a clue
jokes.setup: Abbi arrives at a new scavenger hunt location expecting a clue
jokes.punchline: The stranger delivers an unprompted intimate compliment that could only be Ilana's scripted instruction, delivered by a random person with apparent sincerity
jokes.explanation: The stranger is a surrogate Ilana, and the specific phrasing 'sexy little bitch' is unmistakably Ilana's voice translated through a third party. The rewatch bonus is strong because you appreciate Ilana's coaching labor. Abbi's reaction — 'Where does she find these fucking people?' — is the perfect straight-man button.

jokes.text: Abbi, after being called 'a sexy little bitch' by a stranger, says: 'Where does she find these fucking people?' then: 'Thank you for the information.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has just been complimented/insulted by a stranger clearly prepped by Ilana
jokes.punchline: 'Thank you for the information' — treating the bizarre encounter with bureaucratic politeness, the word 'information' being the funniest choice possible
jokes.explanation: The word 'information' is doing everything. Abbi's polite formality in response to something that requires no formal acknowledgment is perfectly calibrated to her character — she's flustered but too Canadian-polite to be rude. Very high quotability.

jokes.text: Abbi opens a door and immediately says: 'Oh, my God! Hi! Oh... no, please, don't show me your dick! I don't wanna see another dick today!' — only to be told: 'It's a clue.'
jokes.setup: Abbi knocks on a door as part of the scavenger hunt; she greets the person warmly, then immediately panics
jokes.punchline: 'I don't wanna see another dick today' — implying she has already seen at least one unwanted dick today — followed by the deflating reveal that it's just a clue
jokes.explanation: Two-layer joke: the panic itself is funny, but 'another' dick implies a whole unreferenced prior incident. The straight-faced 'It's a clue' deflation is perfectly timed. 'I don't wanna see another dick today' is extremely quotable and does worldbuilding comedy in five words.

jokes.text: Abbi discovers the scavenger hunt ended at the Intrepid. 'Ha-ha-ha! The Intrepid? Beautiful. I mean, I've never... experienced anything like that. I mean, I rode a horse. I have... I didn't know that I could ride a horse.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to express that the scavenger hunt was the most extraordinary experience of her life
jokes.punchline: The most extraordinary thing she can compare it to is discovering mid-scavenger-hunt that she can ride a horse — a revelation delivered as a parenthetical non-sequitur
jokes.explanation: The horse revelation is a perfect non-sequitur because it suggests the scavenger hunt included a horse and Abbi is burying the lede. Abbi's stumbling speech pattern is authentically reproduced from Jacobson's improv style. Ilana's knowing response — 'I did. You know me better than I know myself' — makes the horse real and funnier.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'I did. You know me better than I know myself. Better than you know yourself.' Abbi: 'Oh! Same—' Ilana: 'Page!'
jokes.setup: They're completing each other's sentences as proof of their deep bond
jokes.punchline: They split 'same page' across two people, each saying one word — the joke being that the phrase about being on the same page is itself enacted by being on the same page
jokes.explanation: A structurally elegant meta-joke: the content of the joke IS its form. They demonstrate 'same page' by literally saying the phrase in perfect synchrony. Tight, economical, warmhearted.

jokes.text: 'Funny thing is, we won't have any dreams tonight because we'll be shitting ourselves so crazy with diarrhea.'
jokes.setup: They're having a loving, celebratory moment about their friendship over a beautiful meal
jokes.punchline: Ilana delivers the diarrhea prophecy with the same warmth and certainty as a toast, and Abbi cheerfully responds 'Cheers to that'
jokes.explanation: The joke works because it's a genuine toast — Ilana isn't being gross to be transgressive, she's being honest about the consequences of their favorite meal. 'Shitting ourselves so crazy' is a specific enough phrase to be quotable. Abbi's 'Cheers to that' is the perfect button.

jokes.text: Ilana says 'Happy Friendiversary' and Abbi's reaction is: 'Friendiversary... yeah. Ew.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has just planned an elaborate, loving scavenger hunt for their friendship anniversary
jokes.punchline: The word 'Friendiversary' itself gets an 'Ew' from Abbi — she's embarrassed by the word even as she's clearly moved by the gesture
jokes.explanation: Abbi's instinct to cringe at the sentimentality even while experiencing it is very true to her character and the show's emotional register. The 'Ew' is doing real character work — she's moved but won't admit it easily.

jokes.text: Ilana's gift is a diamond necklace her great-grandmother Fanny Rose smuggled by repeatedly swallowing diamonds, pooping them out, sifting through the poop, and swallowing them again for the entire voyage to America.
jokes.setup: Ilana presents a family heirloom from her great-grandmother during a difficult immigration during 'Fiddler on the Roof times'
jokes.punchline: The heirloom's origin story involves an escalating cycle of swallowing-pooping-sifting-reswallowing diamonds that is presented with complete historical solemnity
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The structure is masterful: it opens with genuine pathos (immigration, violence, shtetl), Grandma Fanny Rose is 'the most innocent-looking,' then pivots to the grotesque logistics with escalating specificity. The phrase 'pooping them out and sifting through the poop and swallowing them again to continue the cycle' is committed, detailed, and absolutely refuses to wink at the audience. The Jewish immigration framing ('Fiddler on the Roof times') is both specific enough to be funny on its own and earns the escalation. This is exceptional character comedy — only Ilana would offer this, only this show would frame it as heroic.

jokes.text: 'It's offensive if you don't accept it. Not just to me but to Grandma Fanny Rose, may she rest in peace.'
jokes.setup: Abbi politely says she can't accept such a significant family heirloom
jokes.punchline: Ilana weaponizes ancestral guilt — invoking the dead grandmother's honor as leverage — with total sincerity
jokes.explanation: 'May she rest in peace' as the button on a guilt-trip about poop diamonds is a perfect callback extension. Ilana's manipulation is so loving it can't be called manipulation. The joke escalates the prior joke without needing to reference the gross details again.

jokes.text: 'It looks gorgeous. Even, like, on a T-shirt, you kinda dressed down, which I get.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is complimenting Abbi on how the necklace looks
jokes.punchline: The compliment is interrupted by a gentle dig about Abbi's casual outfit — 'which I get' softening but not erasing the note
jokes.explanation: A smaller joke but perfectly in character — Ilana's love always comes with unsolicited fashion commentary. 'Which I get' is the funniest two words in the line.

jokes.text: After a dramatic, extended buildup about having researched and thought hard about Ilana's gift, Abbi reveals: 'an intensive hydrating face mask.'
jokes.setup: Abbi builds up the gift with research-based authority and emotional weight — 'I got a memento that I think is the best'
jokes.punchline: The gift is a drugstore face mask — the gap between the setup's gravity and the punchline's bathos is the joke
jokes.explanation: Classic long-buildup/anticlimactic-reveal structure executed cleanly. The length of Abbi's buildup — full of genuine conviction — makes the bathos land harder. The structure is strong; it's not the most original premise but it's very well-executed.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'You feel that I'm dry?' Abbi: 'No. I feel that you are nourishing.' Ilana: '...And that you're extremely rejuvenating for me.'
jokes.setup: Abbi tries to backpedal on the face mask by explaining it as a metaphor for how she feels about Ilana
jokes.punchline: The metaphor produces an increasingly absurd and slightly sexual vocabulary — 'nourishing,' 'rejuvenating,' 'regenerate,' 'restore' — as Abbi digs deeper while Ilana stares
jokes.explanation: An extended cringe sequence that earns each additional beat. The skin-care vocabulary becoming a love language is the joke, and Abbi's desperate sincerity as she reaches for more synonyms ('regenerate,' 'restore') is beautifully awkward. The callback structure of the whole sequence — answering Ilana's 'You feel I'm dry?' with increasing earnestness — adds layers.

jokes.text: Ilana responds to all of Abbi's skin-metaphor love-language with: 'Not mad, just informed.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has bent over backwards to justify the face mask as a deep metaphor; she desperately asks 'Are you mad?'
jokes.punchline: 'Not mad, just informed' — an icily precise response that is both a genuine read of the situation and devastating in its understatement
jokes.explanation: The single best line of the episode. Four words doing the work of a full scene. 'Informed' is the perfect word — it means she now has clarity about how much Abbi cares, and it is not flattering. The phrasing echoes passive-aggressive parental speak ('not angry, just disappointed') but twisted. It's highly quotable in everyday life far beyond this episode, it perfectly crowns a long cringe sequence, and it's completely in Ilana's voice. The economy score is a 10 because no other four words could do this job.

jokes.text: Abbi reveals she planned a big night 'on the top of the Empire State Building!' and also paid the whole bill — immediately followed by Ilana correcting her: 'No, the tradition is that we go Dutch, unless you forgot.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is building up a dramatic, generous gesture — paying the bill and the Empire State Building — as compensation for the weak gift
jokes.punchline: Ilana immediately shuts down the bill-paying with a reminder about the Dutch tradition, turning Abbi's grand gesture into a rule violation
jokes.explanation: The joke is about the friends' financial equality being sacred even when someone is trying to be generous. Ilana's rigidity about the Dutch tradition is both funny and revealing of the friendship's egalitarianism. The rhythm works — Abbi's big announcement deflated in one sentence.

jokes.text: 'Glad I spent 1,200 bucks getting these heads made. What? Forget it.'
jokes.setup: They're at the Empire State Building in the rain; Abbi says she thinks it's 'actually better up here in the rain'
jokes.punchline: Abbi's aside reveals she spent $1,200 on personalized bobbleheads or sculptures as part of the evening — an enormous, absurd expenditure muttered and immediately self-censored
jokes.explanation: A surprise reveal joke executed with masterful casualness. 'Getting these heads made' raises an immediate question about what 'heads' means, the $1,200 price tag is absurdly specific, and 'Forget it' is the perfect shutdown — Abbi catching herself oversharing then retreating. The visual gag of whatever personalized items they're holding at the Empire State Building in the rain adds richness. Very high economy score: eight words that open a world.

jokes.text: At the Empire State Building, they find a telescope and spot a couple having 'hot sex' in a building across the way
jokes.setup: Abbi has planned a romantic city-view activity for their friendiversary
jokes.punchline: The first thing Ilana spots through the telescope is a couple having sex — 'Hot sex! Look right there. Let me see, let me see, let me see' — immediately pivoting the romantic atmosphere
jokes.explanation: A functional setup-for-the-main-plot joke. Ilana's immediate pivot to voyeurism is in character but the joke is fairly predictable given the characters. The value is in what it leads to, not in itself.

jokes.text: Abbi's response to seeing the sex: 'Oh, my God, Ilana, this is fucking disgusting.' Ilana: 'You are so prude.' Abbi: 'It's weird.' Ilana: 'No, I'm not.'
jokes.setup: They're watching people have sex through a telescope
jokes.punchline: Ilana's response to 'You are so prude' being 'No, I'm not' — she hears 'prude' as applying to herself, the misfire revealing how little she can imagine being accused of prudishness
jokes.explanation: The grammar confusion — Ilana responding to 'prude' as if it applied to her — is a quick, sharp character gag. It works because it reveals that Ilana's self-concept as sexually liberated is so total that even mentioning prudishness near her triggers a defensive response.

jokes.text: 'Two ladies? Oh, my God! You gotta see this!'
jokes.setup: They've been watching what appears to be a heterosexual couple through the telescope
jokes.punchline: Ilana discovers the sex involves two women — her excitement escalates beyond the prior level, her enthusiasm being the joke rather than the content
jokes.explanation: The setup-to-escalation works for Ilana's character — her bisexual enthusiasm is a running character trait, not a punchline about the women themselves. The joke is Ilana, not the content.

jokes.text: Abbi sees something alarming through the telescope and says 'What the fuck, Ilana?' — Ilana responds 'Do not be a fucking homophobe.'
jokes.setup: Abbi's alarm at seeing something through the telescope
jokes.punchline: Ilana assumes Abbi is reacting to the lesbian sex and accuses her of homophobia — the real answer is that Abbi is watching someone get thrown off a balcony
jokes.explanation: A structurally excellent misdirection joke. The audience — with Ilana — assumes Abbi is reacting to the lesbian sex, making the homophobia accusation feel reasonable. The reveal that Abbi's actually watching a murder is the genuine twist. Works on rewatch because Ilana's reflexive progressive accusation of Abbi is so perfectly in character and so perfectly wrong.

jokes.text: Police officer responding to the witnessed 'murder': 'Cool. I'm sorry, sweethearts, there's no evidence here. Maybe you two were bored. You just need a little excitement.'
jokes.setup: Abbi and Ilana have just reported witnessing a woman being thrown off a balcony
jokes.punchline: The cop is completely dismissive, suggesting they're just bored women who 'need a little excitement' — his casual sexism played straight
jokes.explanation: A sharp but familiar bit — the cops-don't-believe-women trope played for comedy. 'Cool' as the cop's first response to a murder report is the best word choice here. Serviceable, not exceptional.

jokes.text: Ilana's response to the cop's dismissiveness: 'We don't need any excitement. We were in the middle of excitement.'
jokes.setup: Cop implies they've been making up a crime because they want excitement
jokes.punchline: Ilana's correction — they were already in excitement (the Friendiversary) — is technically accurate and completely misses the point of the cop's condescension
jokes.explanation: A nice quick joke — Ilana literally interpreting 'excitement' and defending the quality of their pre-murder evening. The literalism is funny and in character.

jokes.text: Abbi and Ilana debate whether they actually saw what they saw: 'I mean, we saw that, right?' 'Yeah, I mean, I think so.' — the uncertainty delivered after confidently reporting a murder to police
jokes.setup: They've just finished reporting a definitive murder to the cops
jokes.punchline: The second the cops leave, they begin doubting the very murder they just insisted was real
jokes.explanation: Their immediate doubt after total confidence is true to life and true to these characters — two people who are absolutely certain until they're alone. The small beat is well-observed.

jokes.text: 'He's got a Burton bag! Abbi. Dude. No, the body's in the bag, Ilana!'
jokes.setup: They spot the man from the balcony leaving with a large bag
jokes.punchline: Abbi's leap to 'the body's in the bag' is instant, confident, and completely unverified — her detective reasoning going from zero to murder-conviction in one second
jokes.explanation: The joke is the speed of Abbi's certainty. Works as escalation — she was doubting the murder thirty seconds ago and now she's certain a duffel bag contains a corpse.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'I mean, what are we even gonna say to him when we confront him? / Abbi: 'Yeah, we're gonna be like, we saw you kill your girlfriend, bitch!'
jokes.setup: They're deciding how to approach a presumed murderer
jokes.punchline: Abbi's action-hero confrontation script — 'we saw you kill your girlfriend, BITCH' — is absurdly aggressive and street-confrontational for two women following a man on the street
jokes.explanation: The gap between their terror of the man and the aggressive script Abbi prepares is funny. Abbi temporarily adopting action-hero energy is a nice character inversion — she's usually the cautious one.

jokes.text: Ilana describes her anti-assault strategy: 'I saw this Oprah once where this girl was about to be sexually assaulted, and then she twisted the dude's dick. I mean, that is always in the back of my mind.'
jokes.setup: They're following a suspected murderer and discussing what to do if he has a gun
jokes.punchline: 'That is always in the back of my mind' — the dick-twist is Ilana's active self-defense protocol, kept mentally at the ready, sourced from a specific Oprah episode
jokes.explanation: The specificity of the Oprah sourcing elevates this — it's not just a gross joke, it's a worldview joke. Ilana has a mental library of practical wisdom sourced from daytime television. 'That is always in the back of my mind' delivered as earnest self-defense prep is the perfect button.

jokes.text: Abbi references an Oprah episode from '1998, 12th season' about a woman pissing on her attacker as a self-defense tactic.
jokes.setup: They're discussing self-defense in case the suspect is armed
jokes.punchline: Abbi has memorized the exact season and year of an Oprah episode involving urination as assault defense — and offers it as tactical advice
jokes.explanation: The specificity of '12th season, 1998' is the key — it implies Abbi has retained this specific episode in her tactical-memory bank. Ilana's 'Genius. Yep.' validation makes it better. The detail of the girl twisting a dick as the initial defense strategy escalates appropriately before the urine punch.

jokes.text: They follow the murder suspect — only to discover he's entered a karaoke bar
jokes.setup: They're following a suspected murderer through the city, building tension
jokes.punchline: 'A karaoke bar? What?' — the mundane reveal deflating the thriller tension
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is structural — the mismatch between murder-thriller energy and karaoke bar destination. Functional but not particularly crafted.

jokes.text: 'What kind of sick fuck comes in here and eats dessert after he throws his fiancée out of the building? Why even get engaged if you're just gonna murder her?'
jokes.setup: They're watching the suspect eat dessert at a restaurant
jokes.punchline: Ilana's moral outrage is calibrated entirely wrong — her primary complaint is the bad manners of eating dessert post-murder, and her secondary is the illogic of proposing to someone you'll kill
jokes.explanation: The comedic priority inversion — worrying about the dessert before the murder — is a great Ilana move. 'Why even get engaged if you're just gonna murder her?' as a logical complaint is a dark but well-earned absurdist punchline.

jokes.text: The suspect orders milk. Ilana: 'Ugh, milk? What a fucking baby.'
jokes.setup: They're building a psychological profile of a murderer
jokes.punchline: Ilana's deepest contempt for the suspected murderer is reserved for his beverage choice — milk — not the murder
jokes.explanation: Four words. A suspected murderer's worst attribute, according to Ilana, is ordering milk. The inversion of priorities — milk worse than murder — is a perfect comedic logic inversion. 'What a fucking baby' is extremely quotable and completely transportable to non-murder contexts. High economy.

jokes.text: 'He bought tickets to Daddy's Back.' 'Jerking off at Daddy's Back after he kills his wife? Whoa!'
jokes.setup: They're asking what movie the suspect bought tickets to see, still building their murderer profile
jokes.punchline: Ilana immediately assumes he's going to the movie theater to masturbate, and adds this to the murder as a cumulative crime against decency
jokes.explanation: The leap from 'bought movie tickets' to 'jerking off' is quintessential Ilana logic — she assumes the worst of male behavior without needing evidence. 'Daddy's Back' as the movie title does extra work by being vaguely sexual-sounding, making Ilana's assumption feel almost reasonable.

jokes.text: Theater employee confirms the movie is 1 hour 50 minutes plus '45 minutes of previews.' Abbi immediately calculates this as enough time to break into the apartment.
jokes.setup: They need to know how long the suspect will be occupied
jokes.punchline: The employee casually gives the 45-minute preview estimate as if this is helpful information he always has ready; Abbi uses it for a felony timeline
jokes.explanation: The specificity of '45 minutes of previews' being delivered straight by the theater employee is the funny part — it treats the information as normal and useful. The pivot to apartment-break-in planning using movie previews as cover time is good absurdist logic.

jokes.text: Movie ticket cost: 'An hour and 50 minutes.' 'Plus 45 minutes of previews.'
jokes.setup: Abbi urgently needs the runtime to calculate a break-in window
jokes.punchline: The ticket person volunteers '45 minutes of previews' as additional runtime with the casualness of accepted fact — the previews are now budget, plannable time
jokes.explanation: A small observational joke about movie preview inflation as a reliable budgetary resource. The deadpan delivery of this as a helpful, practical fact is the slight subversion.

jokes.text: Ilana calls Abbi 'a cuckoo bitch' for suggesting they break into the apartment, and Abbi immediately invokes Ilana's own 'we run this city' speech as justification.
jokes.setup: Ilana is suddenly the voice of reason when Abbi proposes the break-in
jokes.punchline: Abbi uses Ilana's own Friendiversary speech about being a 'productive and efficient team' against her to justify committing a felony
jokes.explanation: The role reversal — Ilana as the cautious one, Abbi using Ilana's rhetoric to justify crime — is a structurally satisfying callback. 'The mother of his children does deserve her justice. Her story must be heard' is Abbi doing full Ilana-speak, which makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Whoa! No, bitch. You are a cuckoo bitch.'
jokes.setup: Abbi proposes breaking into the suspect's apartment.
jokes.punchline: Ilana — who has spent the episode doing insane things — draws the line at apartment break-in and calls Abbi a 'cuckoo bitch.' The role reversal is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The joke is the role reversal — Ilana, chaos agent, is calling Abbi insane. 'Cuckoo bitch' as the specific insult is great. The fact that Ilana is the voice of reason here is genuinely funny character inversion.

jokes.text: Abbi convinces the doorman they're the suspect's nieces with an elaborate cover story including a birthday cake — 'We're his nieces. He just left a little bit ago, and we wanted to sneak into his apartment to surprise him for his birthday. See, we got a cake and everything.'
jokes.setup: They need to get into the building to commit a break-in
jokes.punchline: The doorman completely buys this flimsy story because they have a cake
jokes.explanation: Functional plot-comedy. The cake as a social engineering tool is a nice specific detail. The 'Uncle Hudson' improvised name is funny in delivery.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, Uncle Hudson, we love him.' 'Oh, sweet Uncle Hudson. Yeah.'
jokes.setup: They're maintaining the cover story with the doorman
jokes.punchline: The elaboration — both of them individually performing fond memories of an uncle they just invented thirty seconds ago — delivered with complete warmth
jokes.explanation: The callback to the just-established lie with affectionate elaboration is a quick but genuine laugh. 'Sweet Uncle Hudson' is the right level of warmth for a fake uncle.

jokes.text: 'Jesus, white girls can get away with anything if you act dumb enough.' 'I know, right? We're lucky we look dumb.'
jokes.setup: They've just successfully socially-engineered a doorman with a thin story
jokes.punchline: Their meta-commentary on white privilege as a practical tool, delivered as a matter-of-fact observation rather than guilt — and Abbi confirming she considers herself to look dumb
jokes.explanation: A sharp bit of social commentary delivered with Broad City's characteristic nonchalance about structural critique. Ilana naming the mechanism ('white girls who act dumb') and Abbi confirming 'We're lucky we look dumb' — self-aware without being preachy, and landing as genuinely funny rather than lecture-y. High quotability.

jokes.text: The doorman watches them go and says: 'Healthy-looking hookers.'
jokes.setup: Two women have just conned him with a birthday cake story
jokes.punchline: His private assessment — 'Healthy-looking hookers' — as an attempt to reconcile how he was just fooled
jokes.explanation: 'Healthy-looking hookers' is three words doing remarkable work. The adjective 'healthy-looking' is the funny part — he's not even insulting them cleanly, he's somehow admiring their vitality. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: They enter the apartment and Ilana says: 'This was a home that... had heart.'
jokes.setup: They're in the apartment of a man they believe is a murderer
jokes.punchline: Ilana delivers a realtor-speak assessment of the murder apartment with genuine wistfulness
jokes.explanation: The pause before 'had heart' is doing work — Ilana is genuinely moved by the murdered woman's taste in home decor. The past tense 'had' is the key comedic choice, acknowledging the alleged murder implicitly. Economy is very high — five words.

jokes.text: 'Oh, my God. Holy shit! This is an entire accordion folder full of takeout menus.' 'You ever heard of Seamless? Insane, he probably doesn't even let her on the Internet 'cause she'll read too much.'
jokes.setup: They're searching a murder apartment for evidence of the crime
jokes.punchline: The most damning evidence they find is a pre-digital takeout menu folder; Ilana's conspiracy theory explanation is that he controls his partner's internet access
jokes.explanation: Two-layer joke: (1) the absurdist leap from 'accordion folder of takeout menus' to evidence of abuse, and (2) Ilana's specific theory — he bans her from the internet because 'she'll read too much.' That last phrase is brilliantly specific; it implies the woman was intellectually curious and the man was threatened by it. The joke does feminist social commentary while being completely ridiculous.

jokes.text: 'He probably doesn't even let her on the Internet 'cause she'll read too much.'
jokes.setup: The Seamless observation becomes a theory about controlling behavior
jokes.punchline: Ilana's paranoid conspiracy theory about the takeout menus as evidence of Internet control is delivered as logical deduction
jokes.explanation: The leap from menus to Internet control is good paranoid logic. 'She'll read too much' is a nicely specific fear to attribute to the absent murderer. The sex doll doesn't use the Internet, which makes this funnier in retrospect.

jokes.text: 'And all along, his blood is just boiling that she's so much hotter than he is.'
jokes.setup: Looking at a happy couple photo during the 'murder investigation'
jokes.punchline: Ilana's profiling conclusion — fueled by insecurity over her hotness — delivered with total forensic confidence
jokes.explanation: Ilana's murder profiling is built entirely on aesthetic judgments. The specificity of 'his blood is just boiling' makes it feel like she's channeling genuine investigative intuition about hotness hierarchies. Funny, in-character.

jokes.text: 'An entire cabinet full of cleaning supplies? She thinks that she's gonna use them to clean the toilet. Guess what. He's using them to clean up her blood.'
jokes.setup: They find cleaning supplies under the sink
jokes.punchline: Ilana's leap from 'cleaning supplies' to 'blood evidence' via sarcastic dismissal of the innocent explanation
jokes.explanation: 'She thinks that she's gonna use them to clean the toilet. Guess what.' — the rhetorical structure is excellent, setting up a mundane expectation before the dark pivot. The joke is Ilana's forensic confidence and the ridiculous logical chain.

jokes.text: 'They're definitely married! Look at all her clothes! I can't stand dead women's clothes!'
jokes.setup: Finding a closet full of women's clothing in the apartment
jokes.punchline: 'I can't stand dead women's clothes' — Ilana's emotional response to garments that aren't dead women's clothes at all, delivered with genuine distress
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'I can't stand dead women's clothes' is the joke. It's a specific enough sentiment to feel like something Ilana genuinely believes, delivered with real feeling. On rewatch, knowing the clothes belong to sex dolls, this line becomes much funnier. High quotability — very usable in non-murder contexts.

jokes.text: They call 911 while hiding in the closet. Ilana: 'Hey, it's Ilana.' (as if the 911 operator knows her)
jokes.setup: They're hiding in a suspected murderer's closet, calling emergency services
jokes.punchline: 'Hey, it's Ilana' — treating 911 as a personal contact, as if they have a relationship
jokes.explanation: Three words doing perfect character work. 'Hey, it's Ilana' to 911 implies she calls 911 often enough to introduce herself by first name, or that she treats every phone call as a casual social interaction. Completely in character, extremely quotable, near-perfect economy.

jokes.text: 'I'm sorry, I think this is the moment that we die. This is it, this is it. My mom's gonna be so pissed.'
jokes.setup: They believe they're about to be discovered and killed by the murderer
jokes.punchline: Ilana's deepest fear in facing death is that her mom will be upset — delivered not with sadness but as a practical logistical concern
jokes.explanation: 'My mom's gonna be so pissed' as a dying thought is a perfect Ilana joke — it's not that she's scared of death, it's that her mom will be inconvenienced by it. The word 'pissed' rather than 'devastated' or 'heartbroken' does all the work.

jokes.text: Before dying, Abbi confesses: 'I want to tell you something before we die... This morning... I was the horse.'
jokes.setup: They believe they're about to be killed; Abbi makes a deathbed confession to her best friend
jokes.punchline: 'I was the horse' — an unexplained, deeply mysterious confession that references the earlier horse-riding revelation, suggesting Abbi was cosplaying as the horse on the scavenger hunt
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode and one of the best jokes in the series. Everything about this works: (1) It's a deathbed confession, which sets up something profound, (2) it's five syllables that answer the earlier question about the horse while creating ten more questions, (3) Ilana's only response is 'WHAT?' — and it deserves nothing less, (4) the phrase 'I was the horse' is an absurdist masterwork that is simultaneously completely clear (Abbi wore a horse costume) and deeply mysterious (WHY), (5) it pays off a throwaway line from twenty minutes earlier. The callback structure, the economy, the character specificity — this could only happen on this show, to these characters, with this friendship. The callback at the episode's end with 'Forget it! Forget it!' makes it even better. This scores comparably to the vasectomy joke from the anchor examples in terms of structural excellence and cultural imprint, though it's a different kind of joke.

jokes.text: The murderer enters. Abbi immediately offers herself as a sacrifice: 'No, kill me and let her go!' Ilana: 'No, Abbi, no!'
jokes.setup: They've been hiding and are caught; mortal terror
jokes.punchline: Abbi's immediate heroic self-sacrifice impulse — offering to die for Ilana — played completely straight while also being the most dramatic possible response to a man entering his own apartment
jokes.explanation: The selfless sacrifice moment is both genuinely sweet and absurdly dramatic — Abbi has committed to the murder narrative so fully she's doing a hostage movie. The joke is the complete sincerity of both the offer and Ilana's refusal.

jokes.text: The man asks: 'What the fuck is that smell?' Abbi responds: 'Chicken fingers from... The House of Barbecue!'
jokes.setup: They've been discovered hiding in the closet; Terry demands to know what's happening
jokes.punchline: The answer to 'what's that smell' in a murder confrontation is the chicken fingers from their Friendiversary dinner — the episode's throughline suddenly returning as a smell
jokes.explanation: The callback is elegant — the diarrhea prophecy has now manifested as a smell during a hostage situation. 'The House of Barbecue!' delivered with desperate clarifying enthusiasm is Abbi at peak Abbi.

jokes.text: Terry reveals the 'murder victim' was actually a sex doll named Fran, and her 'sister' Lydia is also a sex doll — revealing the entire plot was a misunderstanding about sex dolls
jokes.setup: The entire episode has been building to a murder reveal
jokes.punchline: No murder: Fran is a sex doll. Lydia is also a sex doll. The relationship between a man and his sex doll 'sisters' is the twist
jokes.explanation: The episodic twist is well-executed. The sex doll reveal recontextualizes every piece of 'evidence' collected earlier — the clothes in the closet, the 'body' in the bag, the 'sex' on the balcony. Fran having a sister Lydia who is also a sex doll is an extra layer that makes Terry's life richer and stranger.

jokes.text: 'I didn't push her, she fell! We were making love! And honestly, it's a real design flaw, you know, how heavy they make the head.'
jokes.setup: Terry is defending himself against the accusation of murdering his girlfriend
jokes.punchline: The sex doll fell because of a 'design flaw' in how heavy they make the head — Terry is both confessing to sex doll intimacy and offering a product critique in the same breath
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The delivery is everything: Terry is explaining a product design flaw with the seriousness of a consumer report, while casually revealing he has sex with a doll. 'It's a real design flaw, you know, how heavy they make the head' is a perfect non-sequitur that is both genuinely funny as an observation and deeply sad as character revelation. 'I wanna put the girls up against something, you know, I wanna stand them up straight' — the plural 'girls' doing extra work.

jokes.text: Terry explains the sex doll hospital: 'That's a black market sex doll hospital.' Abbi: 'Like an American Girl doll hospital.' Terry: 'Exactly.'
jokes.setup: Terry explains that he was taking Fran to be repaired after her head fell off
jokes.punchline: Abbi's comparison of a black-market sex doll repair shop to the American Girl doll hospital — the exact right analogy, delivered without irony — and Terry's sincere confirmation
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes of the episode and arguably the season. The American Girl doll hospital comparison is a stroke of brilliance because it is: (1) structurally accurate as an analogy, (2) a collision of wildly incompatible cultural spheres, (3) delivered by Abbi with complete sincerity, and (4) confirmed by Terry with equal sincerity. 'Exactly.' as Terry's response is perfect — he's relieved someone finally understands. The joke rewards cultural knowledge of the American Girl doll hospital (a real, beloved institution), and the parallel is genuinely exact. This is exceptional comedy writing — the kind of specific, strange comparison that feels inevitable once said and impossible before.

jokes.text: 'Then what were you doing jerking off at Daddy's Back?' 'Yeah!' 'I wasn't jerking off. I sometimes go to the movie theater and pay 30 bucks for a ticket because I enjoy the popcorn.'
jokes.setup: They're still interrogating Terry about all the 'evidence'
jokes.punchline: Terry's explanation for the theater is that he pays $30 for a movie ticket — not to watch the movie — because he enjoys the popcorn. A completely new level of sad specificity.
jokes.explanation: Terry's popcorn explanation is a beautifully sad character joke. Paying $30 for a movie ticket you won't watch for the popcorn is a very specific form of lonely man behavior. The joke recontextualizes Terry as not a murderer or pervert but something sadder — a man who has found baroque workarounds for the pleasures of human life. The callback to Ilana's masturbation accusation makes the reversal funnier.

jokes.text: 'Not that a true companion, you know, wasn't injured tonight. No.'
jokes.setup: After the misunderstanding is resolved, they're speaking to the police
jokes.punchline: Ilana's hedged acknowledgment that a sex doll is a 'true companion' — diplomatically honoring Terry's feelings about Fran while technically not saying anything false
jokes.explanation: Ilana's diplomatic care for Terry's relationship with Fran is both funny and touching. 'A true companion' used sincerely to describe a sex doll, with no irony, is pure Broad City — sex-positive, judgment-free, and somehow completely correct.

jokes.text: 'And what's the difference between Fran and your guns making you feel like men? And what's the difference between Fran and... this guy? If I rubbed him on my clit, does that make me a fucking freak?'
jokes.setup: The police are treating the sex doll situation as something shameful
jokes.punchline: Ilana delivers a full feminist defense of Terry's sex doll use, including demonstrating by considering rubbing a decorative object on her genitals as a thought experiment
jokes.explanation: A genuinely transgressive feminist speech that's also extremely funny. The progression — guns to sex dolls to personal demonstration — is escalation comedy done right. 'He's soft and firm at the same time. It's an interesting feeling I wouldn't mind experiencing' is Ilana being genuinely curious rather than performatively transgressive, which makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Ilana says 'You said disgusting things to me.' — referring to something Abbi apparently said during a terrifying confrontation
jokes.setup: They've just survived a near-death experience
jokes.punchline: Ilana's complaint about the experience is that Abbi 'said disgusting things' — not that they were almost killed, but that Abbi's language was inappropriate
jokes.explanation: The inversion of priorities — Ilana's main complaint post-confrontation is Abbi's language — is a quick character beat. Slightly undercut by being unclear what specifically was said, but the structure works.

jokes.text: 'Bye, Fran.' (Ilana says goodbye to the sex doll as they leave the apartment)
jokes.setup: They're leaving Terry's apartment after the misunderstanding is resolved
jokes.punchline: Ilana says goodbye to the sex doll personally — treating Fran as a social acquaintance worthy of a direct farewell
jokes.explanation: Two words. The maximum possible economy for a joke about being emotionally generous to a sex doll. 'Bye, Fran' treats Fran as a social equal, which is exactly what Ilana's worldview demands. Very high quotability precisely because of its absurd brevity.

jokes.text: Terry, alone after they've left: 'Well, Fran... it really is my birthday.'
jokes.setup: The girls used a birthday as their cover story for the doorman; Terry has been left alone with Fran
jokes.punchline: Terry reveals — or pretends — it actually is his birthday, sharing the moment with his sex doll in an aside that is both deeply sad and perfect
jokes.explanation: A perfect coda. Every element earns its place: (1) it's a callback to the birthday cake cover story, (2) 'Well, Fran...' positions Fran as the only person Terry has to share this with, (3) the reveal that it actually is his birthday is both devastating and hilarious — reality has mimicked their lie, (4) he's telling a sex doll about his birthday with the intimate tone of a confession. The joke is doing enormous emotional and comedic work in seven words. It's one of the most complete small moments in the episode, achieving the rare comedy feat of being funny and genuinely poignant simultaneously.

jokes.text: Someone offers a bite of something in the aftermath; Ilana says: 'Okay, Alec Baldwin. Oof. No shoes. Yeah. I feel like he gets pedicures and keeps them nice, right?'
jokes.setup: Transitioning to a calmer conversation game — apparently rating celebrity feet
jokes.punchline: They're playing a 'celebrity feet: shoes on or shoes off?' game with complete seriousness, immediately after a hostage situation
jokes.explanation: The abrupt transition to an extremely specific celebrity foot-aesthetics debate is quintessential Broad City — these two going from near-death to niche games without pause. The game itself is funny by virtue of its specificity: not 'hot or not' but specifically 'shoes on or off, feet wise.'

jokes.text: Abbi: 'People get fat injected to the balls of their feet to make high heels easier to wear.' Ilana: 'Ugh, gross.' Abbi: 'I know, um...'
jokes.setup: Discussing whether Kim Kardashian has had foot work done
jokes.punchline: Abbi casually drops the fact of foot-ball fat injection as common knowledge — and both women are equally horrified despite having just been through an actual hostage situation
jokes.explanation: The foot-ball fat injection is a real thing and Abbi's casual knowledge of it is funny. Ilana's 'Ugh, gross' following immediately after she's defended sex dolls is a nice priority inversion.

jokes.text: 'Ugh, dude. Michael Jordan. Hmm, okay. Yikes. Well, I feel like his feet would be, like, super scary. Horrifying. But so scary that I'd want to see them, so no shoes, definitely.'
jokes.setup: Continuing the celebrity feet game
jokes.punchline: Michael Jordan's feet are so terrifying that the correct choice is still 'no shoes' — because fear itself is a form of desire in this framework
jokes.explanation: The logic of 'so scary I have to see them' is a genuinely funny escalation of the foot-game framework. The progression from 'scary' to 'horrifying' to 'no shoes, definitely' is well-structured.

jokes.text: Abbi confesses she forgot the Friendiversary: 'I forgot about our friendiversary. I'm sorry, I... I'm really sorry.' Ilana: 'Yeah, I know.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has been carrying guilt about the face mask gift all episode
jokes.punchline: 'Yeah, I know.' — Ilana has known all along; she did the scavenger hunt precisely because she knew Abbi forgot
jokes.explanation: 'Yeah, I know.' is the right two words. It recasts the entire episode — Ilana built the scavenger hunt specifically because she knew her friend would forget, and she loved her anyway. The joke is both funny and warm. On rewatch, the scavenger hunt reads differently knowing this.

jokes.text: 'But that doesn't mean you should die.' — Ilana's assessment of the proportionality of Abbi's crime
jokes.setup: They're discussing the forgetting of the Friendiversary
jokes.punchline: 'But that doesn't mean you should die' — Ilana clarifying that, while forgetting is bad, it is not a capital offense
jokes.explanation: A perfectly calibrated deadpan that references the nearly dying while measuring it against forgetting an anniversary. The implication that the murder threat was a proportionate response — briefly — before Ilana reassures her it wasn't, is very funny. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Abbi doubles down on the sheet mask: 'I will say that I do really stand by those sheet masks, like, I know it's a small thing, but those are very hydrating... it's just a great product overall, I think, in my opinion.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has graciously accepted the face mask despite it being a terrible gift
jokes.punchline: Abbi, instead of leaving it behind, doubles down with product evangelism — 'a great product overall, I think, in my opinion' hedges the claim so thoroughly it undercuts itself
jokes.explanation: Abbi's inability to let the face mask go is excellent character comedy — she's trying to retroactively earn the gift by endorsing the product. The triple hedge ('I think, in my opinion') is the tell. 'Sure.' as Ilana's response is deadpan perfection.

jokes.text: Abbi's defense of the sheet masks: 'I will say that I do really stand by those sheet masks, like, I know it's a small thing, but those are very hydrating. It's just a great product overall, I think, in my opinion.'
jokes.setup: They're wrapping up the gift exchange conversation; Abbi already admitted the masks were a terrible gift
jokes.punchline: Abbi can't let go of the sheet masks — doubling down on their quality as a consumer product even while conceding they were a bad Friendiversary gift
jokes.explanation: 'I think, in my opinion' — the doubling of 'I think' and 'in my opinion' as Abbi backs herself into a corner is a perfect speech pattern. She's simultaneously admitting fault and defending the product. Very Abbi.

jokes.text: 'All the witch energy we have soaked up from this city.'
jokes.setup: Reflecting on how powerful they've become together
jokes.punchline: 'Witch energy' as the explanation for their strength — delivered as factual, not metaphorical
jokes.explanation: 'Witch energy' as a genuine belief system expressed in a single phrase is pure Ilana voice. The idea that the city has depositable magic that you can absorb is both funny and somehow not wrong.

jokes.text: 'Also, okay, let's just say that that dude had murdered his wife tonight. Right. And let's just say that she was a human. Yes. I mean, we would have solved a fucking murder mystery tonight.'
jokes.setup: Reflecting on their detective work
jokes.punchline: 'Let's just say that she was a human' — the conditional they have to add, acknowledging the sex doll twist, as a hypothetical for the murder mystery they almost solved
jokes.explanation: 'And let's just say that she was a human' is an excellent callback joke — the necessary qualifier after the sex doll reveal, delivered with Abbi's earnest 'Yes' as agreement. The bar they've set for their own detective triumph includes the sub-clause that the victim be a person.

jokes.text: 'How are we not magic? You tell me, 'cause I don't know. You can't tell me. You don't know!'
jokes.setup: Mutual escalating amazement at themselves
jokes.punchline: Ilana establishing that the question is rhetorical and unanswerable in the same breath — 'You can't tell me. You don't know!'
jokes.explanation: A functional energy joke. The character energy is right — this is how these two talk about themselves — but it's not particularly crafted. It rides performance rather than writing.

jokes.text: Abbi starts to confess 'I was the horse' again — Ilana immediately: 'Forget it! Forget it! Forget it. Forget it.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is about to re-explain the horse confession from the closet
jokes.punchline: Ilana has processed the horse revelation and her immediate instinct is to shut it down completely — 'Forget it' four times, with escalating urgency
jokes.explanation: The closing callback to 'I was the horse' is as good as the original joke. Ilana shutting it down with four 'Forget it's, each slightly different in urgency, is the perfect cap for the episode's best joke. Structurally, this is the ideal callback execution: you don't need to explain the joke again, you just need to confirm the audience remembers it, and Ilana's panic does exactly that. Paired with 'But can we do one more?' to end on warmth — perfect episode structure. This scores equally high because it extends the original joke's life without diluting it.
